questions.question: Explain the difference between a Max Heap and a Min Heap with examples.
keyPoints: Max Heap: Parent >= Children, maximum at root
Min Heap: Parent <= Children, minimum at root
Both satisfy heap property but with opposite ordering
Example: Max heap [50,30,20], Min heap [10,25,30]

questions.question: Describe the array representation of a heap. Given parent at index i, write formulas for finding left child, right child, and parent index.
keyPoints: Heaps are stored as complete binary trees in arrays
Left Child Index: 2i + 1
Right Child Index: 2i + 2
Parent Index: (i-1)/2 or floor((i-1)/2)
Root is at index 0

questions.question: Explain the Heapify operation. How does it differ when used top-down vs bottom-up?
keyPoints: Heapify maintains heap property by comparing node with children
Top-down (percolate down): Used after extracting max/min
Bottom-up: Used when building heap from array
Time complexity: O(log n) for single node

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert an element into a max heap and explain its time complexity.
keyPoints: Step 1: Insert new element at the end of array
Step 2: Compare with parent
Step 3: If larger than parent, swap and repeat
Step 4: Continue until heap property satisfied or root reached
Time Complexity: O(log n)

questions.question: What are the applications of heap data structure? Mention at least four real-world applications.
keyPoints: Priority Queue implementation
Heap Sort algorithm
Dijkstra's Shortest Path Algorithm
Prim's Minimum Spanning Tree
Kth largest/smallest element finding
Task scheduling in operating systems
Median maintenance in data streams

questions.question: Explain the Build Heap process. Why is it more efficient to start heapifying from index n/2 - 1?
keyPoints: Build Heap converts unsorted array to heap
Start from n/2 - 1 down to 0
Elements from n/2 to n-1 are leaves (no children)
Leaf nodes are already heap of size 1
Total time complexity: O(n) despite each heapify being O(log n)

questions.question: Trace through the Heap Sort algorithm step by step to sort the array [4, 10, 3, 5, 1]. Show all steps including heap building and repeated extraction.
keyPoints: Build max-heap: [10,5,3,4,1]
Extract max (10), swap with last: [1,5,3,4|10]
Heapify from root: [5,4,3,1|10]
Continue until sorted: [1,3,4,5,10]
Explain each swap and heapify operation
Time complexity: O(n log n)

questions.question: Compare and contrast Quick Sort and Heap Sort. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Heap Sort: O(n log n) worst-case, O(1) space, not stable
Quick Sort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst-case, O(log n) space, not stable
Heap Sort is in-place sorting algorithm
Quick Sort typically faster in practice due to cache locality
Heap Sort preferred when: worst-case guarantee needed, O(1) space required
Quick Sort preferred when: average case performance matters, data fits in memory

questions.question: Design an algorithm using heap to find the median of a streaming set of numbers. Explain with an example and analyze time complexity.
keyPoints: Use two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half
Balance condition: sizes differ by at most 1
Median is average of tops if equal, or top of larger heap
Insert: O(log n), Get Median: O(1)
Example: 5,15,1,3 → after balancing: max-heap[5,3], min-heap[15], median=5

questions.question: Explain how heaps are used in Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm. Why is a binary heap preferred over a simple array for the priority queue?
keyPoints: Dijkstra uses min-heap to always extract vertex with minimum distance
Initialize distances: source=0, others=∞
Extract min, relax edges, update heap
Array: O(V) for extract-min, O(1) for decrease-key
Binary Heap: O(log V) for extract-min, O(log V) for decrease-key
For sparse graphs (E << V²), binary heap is significantly faster
Total: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap vs O(V²) with array
